planting.note: A large-flowered vegetative blue planted from rooted starts.

directions: Set out rooted cuttings in full sun after the last frost date. Keep the soil evenly moist to establish, then water moderately through summer. Large deep-blue flowers crowd tall spikes and never need deadheading.

planting.note: A soft steel-blue bicolor grown from cuttings.

directions: Plant rooted starts after frost in a sunny, well-drained spot. Water at the base and feed monthly for tall, well-branched spikes. Muted steel-blue and white flowers bloom steadily through heat and humidity.

planting.note: A trailing, spreading selection ideal for baskets and containers.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings along basket edges or the front of containers after frost. Water regularly while it fills in, then keep evenly moist in summer heat. Blue spikes spill over the sides and bloom continuously without deadheading.

planting.note: A light powder-blue vegetative variety planted from cuttings.

directions: Transplant rooted starts into warm, sunny beds after all frost passes. Give average water and a monthly feeding for full, upright spikes. Pale powder-blue flowers cover tall stems from summer into fall.

planting.note: A large-flowered vegetative series planted after frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into sunny, well-drained soil once nights stay warm. Water to establish, then keep moderately moist and feed monthly. Broad deep-blue flowers pack the spikes and bloom without any deadheading.

planting.note: Large-flowered vegetative white for beds and containers.

directions: Plant rooted starts at their original depth after the last frost. Give full sun, steady moisture to establish, and light monthly feeding. Big clean-white flowers crowd the spikes from early summer to frost.

planting.note: A rare warm-toned angelonia grown from rooted cuttings.

directions: Transplant rooted starts into full sun after frost danger has passed. Water evenly to establish, then allow the surface to dry between drinks. Deep cherry-red flowers stand out on the spikes all season without deadheading.

planting.note: A saturated rose vegetative selection planted after frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into warm, sunny, well-drained soil. Keep evenly moist while establishing and feed lightly each month. Rich rose-pink flowers fill the large spikes from summer through fall.

planting.note: Large-flowered vegetative pink for the warm season.

directions: Plant rooted starts in full sun after the last spring frost. Water regularly to establish, then keep moderately moist through heat. Soft pink flowers cover the broad spikes and need no deadheading.

planting.note: A deep purple vegetative variety grown from cuttings.

directions: Transplant rooted starts into sunny, well-drained beds after frost. Provide even moisture to establish and a monthly feeding for full spikes. Large deep-purple flowers bloom continuously through summer heat.

planting.note: A bright raspberry vegetative selection planted after frost.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into full sun once nights are reliably warm. Water to establish, then keep the soil evenly but not constantly moist. Vivid raspberry flowers fill the large spikes from early summer to frost.

planting.note: A vegetative upright series planted from rooted cuttings.

directions: Plant rooted starts in full sun after all danger of frost. Water evenly while establishing and feed monthly for tall, sturdy spikes. Deep purple flowers bloom nonstop and make long-lasting cut stems.

planting.note: A soft lavender vegetative selection set out after frost.

directions: Transplant rooted cuttings into warm, sunny, well-drained soil. Keep the roots moist to establish, then water moderately through summer. Airy lavender spikes bloom steadily and hold well as cut flowers.

planting.note: A white vegetative variety grown from rooted cuttings.

directions: Set rooted starts in full sun after the last frost of spring. Water regularly to establish, then let the surface dry slightly between drinks. Clean white spikes bloom all summer and cut well for arrangements.

planting.note: A seed-raised series with early, uniform flowering.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on warm mix and keep it bright and moist until it sprouts. Move seedlings to full sun after the last frost and feed lightly. Rose-toned spikes bloom early on a compact, weather-resistant plant.

planting.note: A spreading vegetative selection well suited to baskets.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings near container edges in full sun after frost. Water to establish and feed monthly for a full, mounding spread. Purple spikes trail and bloom continuously right up to first frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost and set young plants out as the ground warms.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seeds and press them into moist mix without covering, since light aids germination. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching, and stake taller stems to keep the spikes upright. Flowering begins in cool weather and continues until summer heat slows the plant; shear spent spikes to prompt a second flush.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and move them out around the last frost for tall cutting spikes.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a fine, damp mix and keep it bright and evenly moist until sprouts appear. This tall series thrives with support netting or staking as the flower spikes lengthen. Cut stems for the vase when the lowest few florets have opened for the longest display.

planting.note: Start early indoors; this heat-tolerant cut-flower group performs into warmer weather.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and leave it uncovered under bright light. Grow with vertical netting because the long, sturdy stems are bred for florists' bunches. Harvest when a third of the florets on the spike have opened to enjoy the fullest bloom in the vase.

planting.note: Start indoors for early transplants of these double, azalea-flowered spikes.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and keep it lit and damp, as burying it prevents sprouting. The open-faced double florets lack the classic snapping jaw but make ruffled, long-lasting cut spikes. Support the stems and cut them for arrangements once the bottom blooms unfurl.

planting.note: Sow early indoors for open-throated butterfly-type cut flowers.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist medium and mist gently to keep the seed coat damp during germination. These loose, open-faced blooms are prized by flower farmers for their soft colors and vase life. Cut spikes when a few lower florets show color, and net the row to keep stems straight.

planting.note: Begin indoors; this tall florist group needs a long, cool growing stretch.

directions: Leave seed uncovered on damp mix and hold it under bright light until seedlings emerge. Grown for cutting, the long single stems benefit from one layer of support netting. Harvest spikes with the lower third in bloom for maximum shelf life.

planting.note: Start indoors for tall, warm-tolerant cutting spikes.

directions: Press the dust-fine seed onto moist mix without covering and keep it warm and bright. This vigorous group holds up to heat better than most, extending the cutting season. Net the stems for straightness and pick spikes as the base florets open.

planting.note: Raise indoors for mid-height plants that suit both borders and cutting.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the seed lit and damp until it germinates in a week or two. These branching mid-size plants bloom early and hold up well in wind and rain. Deadhead finished spikes to keep fresh flowers coming through the cool season.

planting.note: Start indoors for uniform mid-height bedding and cutting plants.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist mix and do not cover, since these seeds need light. The well-branched plants produce full spikes early in the season across a wide color range. Cut or deadhead spent stems to encourage repeat flowering into summer.

planting.note: Start early indoors for mid-height, early-blooming bedding spikes.

directions: Press seed onto damp mix and hold it in bright light until seedlings show. This early, compact group flowers reliably in cool spring conditions. Shear back after the first flush to freshen the plants for a later round of bloom.

planting.note: Raise indoors for well-branched mid-size garden plants.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface and keep it moist and lit for even germination. The bushy plants send up many secondary spikes for a long garden show. Remove faded spikes to redirect energy into new flowering shoots.

planting.note: Start indoors for fast, dwarf bedding plants that need no pinching.

directions: Surface-sow onto damp mix and keep it bright until the seedlings emerge. This dwarf, early series branches on its own and blooms quickly in packs or borders. Trim off spent flowers to keep the tidy mounds blooming through spring.

planting.note: Start indoors for very early, compact container and bedding plants.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist mix and leave it uncovered under light. This is one of the earliest dwarf series, blooming fast in small pots and edging. Pinch off tired spikes to prompt fresh side shoots and longer color.

planting.note: Start indoors for dwarf, double azalea-type flowers in beds and pots.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the seed damp and lit until sprouts appear. The open, double florets give a ruffled look on tidy dwarf plants that tolerate heat well. Deadhead spent stems to keep the mounds flowering through the season.

planting.note: Start indoors for very compact, early-flowering dwarf plants.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface and keep it moist under bright light. This tightly dwarf series is among the earliest to bloom in cell packs. Snip off faded spikes to keep the small plants covered in flowers.

planting.note: Start indoors for rust-tolerant dwarf bedding plants.

directions: Surface-sow onto damp mix and hold under light until germination. This base-branching dwarf series carries good tolerance to rust disease. Keep water off the foliage where possible and deadhead to extend the bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors for early, uniform dwarf plants in packs and edging.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto moist mix and leave it exposed to light. This early dwarf series flowers uniformly and stays neat in containers. Remove finished spikes to keep the compact plants in continuous color.

planting.note: Start indoors for cascading plants suited to baskets and window boxes.

directions: Surface-sow onto damp mix and keep it bright until seedlings appear. Unlike upright types, this series spills over the edges of hanging baskets and walls. Trim back long trails lightly to encourage branching and fresh flower spikes.

planting.note: Start indoors for intermediate garden and cut-flower spikes.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep the seed damp and lit for even sprouting. These vigorous mid-height plants branch freely for repeated garden bloom. Deadhead or cut spikes as lower florets open to keep new stems forming.

planting.note: Start indoors for tall, scented cutting stems.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix without covering and give it bright light. This series is notable for fragrant flowers on long cutting stems. Net the row for support and cut spikes as the base florets open to enjoy the scent indoors.

planting.note: Start indoors; this tall florist series is grown for greenhouse and field cutting.

directions: Surface-sow onto fine damp mix and hold under bright light until seedlings emerge. Bred for professional cut-flower growers, the long single stems need one layer of support netting. Harvest with the lowest florets open for the best vase performance.

planting.note: Start indoors for tall, uniform cutting spikes.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp mix and keep it lit and moist during germination. This tall series produces straight florist stems when grown with support netting. Cut spikes as the base florets open to maximize their days in the vase.

planting.note: Start indoors for a dark-flowered, bronze-leaved heirloom.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the seed damp and lit until it germinates. This heirloom pairs deep crimson flowers with dark bronze-green foliage for strong contrast. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the striking plants blooming through cool weather.

planting.note: Start indoors for a bicolor heirloom with crimson-and-white flowers.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of moist mix and give it bright light to sprout. Each flower shows a deep crimson face over a near-white throat for a two-tone effect. Remove faded spikes to encourage a long run of the eye-catching blooms.

planting.note: Start indoors for tall, open-faced butterfly-type flowers.

directions: Surface-sow onto damp mix and keep it bright until seedlings appear. The open, five-lobed florets resemble small azaleas rather than the usual snapping mouth. Support the tall stems and cut or deadhead to prolong the flowering display.

planting.note: Start indoors for a low, spreading dwarf mix for edging and rockeries.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface and keep it moist under light. This low, mounding mix carpets edges and rock gardens with mixed colors. Shear lightly after the first bloom to freshen the plants for more flowers.

planting.note: Start indoors for soft pink-and-white cutting spikes.

directions: Press seed onto damp mix without covering and keep it bright to germinate. The blush pink and cream flowers are a longtime favorite for soft-toned bouquets. Net the stems for support and cut spikes as the lower florets open.

planting.note: Start indoors for pure white, lightly scented cutting stems.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and hold it under bright light until sprouting. The crisp white flowers carry a gentle fragrance and suit wedding arrangements. Support the tall stems and harvest as the base florets begin to open.

planting.note: Start indoors for a variegated-leaf variety with pink flowers.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it damp and lit until seedlings appear. This selection is grown as much for its cream-edged variegated foliage as its pink blooms. Deadhead spent spikes to keep both the leaves and flowers looking fresh.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after frost; this species overwinters in mild zones.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it lit and just moist until it germinates over a couple of weeks. This tougher, short-lived perennial species carries pale yellow flowers and returns for several seasons in well-drained soil. Cut back after flowering to keep the clump tidy and encourage more bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors for a trailing alpine species for walls and rock gardens.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a gritty, free-draining mix and keep it lightly moist under bright light. This low, spreading species with soft hairy leaves drapes over walls and stones in dry sites. Trim after flowering to keep the trailing mats compact.

planting.note: Start indoors for a compact rock-garden species tolerant of dry heat.

directions: Surface-sow into a gritty mix and keep it just damp under light until seedlings emerge. This dwarf species bears small pale pink to white flowers on gray-green mounds and thrives in lean, sharply drained soil. Shear spent stems to prolong the summer flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors for a Western native species suited to dry, open sites.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep it lightly moist under bright light, as germination can be slow. This tall, sticky-stemmed California native carries rose-pink flowers and flourishes on dry, sunny slopes, especially after fire. Water sparingly once established and leave seed heads for self-sowing.

planting.note: Start indoors for compact, well-branched bedding plants.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp mix and leave it uncovered under light. This compact series branches well and offers a broad range of clear colors and bicolors. Remove spent spikes to keep the bushy plants flowering through the cool months.

planting.note: Start indoors for early, base-branching dwarf plants.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and keep it bright until seedlings appear. This early dwarf series branches from the base for full, compact plants in packs and edging. Deadhead finished spikes to keep the small mounds in bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors for a low evergreen alpine species for walls and troughs.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a gritty mix and keep it just moist under light until germination. This spreading, semi-evergreen species bears small white flowers veined with pink on hairy gray foliage. Give it sharp drainage and trim lightly after bloom to keep the mats neat.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors 10 to 12 weeks before planting, pressing it onto the surface of a warm, moist tray. This bronze-leaved dwarf carries white blooms that contrast with the dark foliage and tolerates more sun than green-leaved types. Keep soil evenly moist and expect flowers from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Move to the garden once frost danger ends.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of a warm tray and keep humid until sprouts appear. Bronze foliage sets off soft pink flowers on this compact bedding series member. Plant in fertile, moist soil and enjoy nonstop bloom until the first autumn frost.

planting.note: Transplant outdoors after the last frost date.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist medium indoors and hold at room temperature until germination. This bronze-leaved selection bears crisp white blooms and handles full sun better than green forms. Water steadily and it flowers freely from summer to frost.

planting.note: Set out after nights have warmed past frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed indoors and keep warm and bright until seedlings establish. Bronze foliage frames white-edged pink blooms on this dwarf mounding plant. Plant in rich, moist soil for constant color through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant out once soil warms and frost has passed.

directions: Barely press the seed onto a moist surface and grow warm under bright light. This bronze-leaved dwarf produces rose-pink flowers and stands up to summer sun. Keep the root zone evenly damp for uninterrupted flowering until frost.

planting.note: Move transplants outdoors after the frost-free date.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of a warm, moist tray about three months ahead of planting. Green-leaved plants in this vigorous series carry pure white blooms and knit into an even bedding carpet. Give steady moisture and light shade in hot climates for bloom all season.

planting.note: Plant out when frost danger has passed.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto moist mix indoors and keep humid until germination. Green foliage carries rose-pink blooms on this dependable, uniform bedding series. Plant in well-drained fertile soil and water consistently for continuous summer color.

planting.note: Transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and grow warm and bright until plants are sturdy. This green-leaved selection bears bright scarlet-red flowers over neat mounds. Set into rich soil after frost and keep moist for bloom until autumn.

planting.note: Set out after the frost-free date arrives.

directions: Start the fine seed indoors on the surface of a warm tray and keep humid. Bronze foliage sets off red blooms on this early, uniform series bred for bedding. Water steadily and give some afternoon shade where summers are hot.

planting.note: Plant outdoors once frost risk ends.

directions: Press seed onto moist medium and grow warm until seedlings can be pricked out. This bronze-leaved plant carries white blooms that stand out against the dark leaves. Keep evenly moist in fertile soil for reliable color through the season.

planting.note: Move plants out after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the dustlike seed indoors and keep warm and evenly moist. Large red flowers cover green foliage on this early-blooming, weather-tolerant series. Plant in well-drained soil after frost and water regularly for all-season bloom.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost danger has passed.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of a warm, humid tray several weeks before planting. Green-leaved plants produce clean white blooms on tidy, uniform mounds ideal for edging. Keep the soil moist and lightly shaded in hot regions for continuous flowering.

planting.note: Plant out once the risk of frost is gone.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix indoors and grow warm under bright light. Green foliage carries scarlet blooms on this vigorous, well-branched bedding series. Give steady moisture and it flowers heavily from summer through frost.

planting.note: Set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed indoors and keep warm and humid until sprouts appear. This bronze-leaved series member bears rose-pink flowers and tolerates heat and sun. Plant in fertile, moist soil for dependable bloom all summer.

planting.note: Transplant outdoors after frost has passed.

directions: Start seed on the surface of a warm, moist tray indoors and keep bright. Green foliage carries fully double, rosebud-like pink flowers on compact plants. Keep evenly moist and enjoy the ruffled blooms from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant out after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors many weeks ahead and keep warm, humid, and lit until seedlings form. This series pairs dark bronze foliage with large double scarlet flowers on well-branched plants. Grow in filtered shade with rich, moist soil for heavy bloom from summer to frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors once frost season ends.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a warm tray indoors and hold under bright light until seedlings develop. Green foliage carries large double yellow blooms on compact, upright plants suited to beds and containers. Keep soil evenly moist in dappled shade for nonstop flowering through summer.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost date.

directions: Start the tiny seed indoors on a warm, humid surface and keep brightly lit. This selection bears large double white flowers over green foliage on tidy mounded plants. Grow in filtered light with rich, moist soil for continuous bloom until frost.

planting.note: Set plants out after frost danger has ended.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep warm and moist until seedlings can be transplanted. Large double orange blooms rise above green foliage on this compact, free-flowering series. Plant in dappled shade with fertile, moist soil for color all season long.

planting.note: Plant out once nights are frost-free.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist medium indoors and grow warm under bright light. Double cream blooms edged in rose sit above green foliage on this bicolor selection. Keep evenly moist in filtered shade for showy, ruffled flowers through summer.

planting.note: Move to hanging baskets outdoors after frost.

directions: Start seed indoors on a warm, humid surface and grow brightly lit until transplant size. This pendulous type cascades with double orange blooms and suits hanging baskets and window boxes. Keep the mix evenly moist in filtered light for trailing color all summer.

planting.note: Set baskets outdoors once frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed indoors and keep warm and moist until seedlings establish. Trailing stems carry double rose-pink flowers that spill gracefully over container edges. Grow in dappled shade with steady moisture for cascading bloom through the season.

planting.note: Plant out after the frost-free date.

directions: Press the fine seed onto a warm, moist surface indoors and keep well lit. Dark bronze foliage contrasts with large double yellow blooms on this compact series. Grow in filtered shade with rich, moist soil for heavy flowering until frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors when frost risk has ended.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface of a warm, humid tray and keep brightly lit. Large double deep-red blooms rise over green foliage on this uniform, compact series. Keep soil evenly moist in dappled light for nonstop color through summer.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Start seed indoors on a warm, humid surface and grow brightly lit until transplant size. This vigorous boliviensis selection carries masses of pendant orange-red trumpet flowers on cascading stems. Give filtered sun and steady moisture for heat-proof bloom that spills from baskets all summer.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out after all frost has passed.

directions: Set nursery-grown plants into rich, well-drained soil or baskets once nights stay mild. Cascading stems drip with fiery orange-red pendant flowers that thrive in heat and resist wilting. Water evenly and give filtered sun for continuous bloom until the first frost.

planting.note: Plant out after frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Set transplants into fertile, well-drained soil or large containers after the last frost. Glossy angel-wing foliage arches above pendant clusters of red flowers on this vigorous hybrid. Water steadily and it blooms heavily in sun or part shade from summer until frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors after the frost-free date.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in rich, well-drained soil or roomy pots once frost has passed. Large glossy wing-shaped leaves set off drooping sprays of pink blooms on robust, arching stems. Keep evenly moist and it flowers freely in sun to part shade all season.

common_name: Whopper Red with Green Leaf Begonia
scientific_name: Begonia x benariensis 'Whopper Red Green Leaf'

planting.note: Set plants out after all danger of frost.

directions: Plant transplants into rich, well-drained soil or large containers once nights are warm. This oversized hybrid makes a shrub-sized mound of green foliage topped with big red blooms. Water evenly and it performs in sun or shade with continuous flowering until frost.

common_name: Whopper Rose with Bronze Leaf Begonia
scientific_name: Begonia x benariensis 'Whopper Rose Bronze Leaf'

planting.note: Transplant outdoors after frost has cleared.

directions: Set rooted plants into fertile, moist, well-drained soil once the weather warms. Dark bronze leaves form a large mound topped with big rose-pink flowers on this vigorous hybrid. Give even moisture in sun or part shade for bold color all season.

common_name: BIG Red with Bronze Leaf Begonia
scientific_name: Begonia x benariensis 'Big Red Bronze Leaf'

planting.note: Plant out once frost season has ended.

directions: Set transplants into rich, well-drained soil or containers after the last frost. This large landscape begonia carries red blooms above bronze foliage and thrives in sun or shade. Water evenly and it flowers nonstop from planting until frost with no deadheading needed.

common_name: BIG Rose with Green Leaf Begonia
scientific_name: Begonia x benariensis 'Big Rose Green Leaf'

planting.note: Move outdoors after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into fertile, moist soil or large pots once frost has passed. Big rose-pink flowers rise over glossy green foliage on this weather-tough landscape hybrid. Keep evenly watered in sun to shade for constant bloom through the growing season.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers in spring in a sheltered, shaded spot.

directions: Set tubers a couple of inches deep in humus-rich, moist soil in spring under light shade. This is the one reliably winter-hardy begonia, dying back each fall and returning from tubers and leaf bulbils in spring. Enjoy pink blooms with red-veined leaves from late summer into autumn, and mulch the crown in colder zones.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors in early spring and plant out after frost danger passes.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on moist mix and press in lightly rather than burying it deeply. Grow on in bright light and move to a sunny bed or container once settled weather arrives. Expect a mound of star-shaped lavender-blue daisies with dark centers through the warm months.

planting.note: Sow indoors six weeks before the last frost for early color.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a fine seed mix and keep just moist at warm room temperature. Harden off seedlings and plant into well-drained, sunny ground or pots. Shear lightly after the first flush to keep the deep violet-blue daisies coming.

planting.note: Start under cover in spring and transplant once nights stay mild.

directions: Barely cover the seed and maintain steady moisture until germination. Give plants full sun and gritty, free-draining soil so the roots never stay soggy. Clean white daisies with yellow eyes appear through summer and last longer with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Grow seedlings indoors and set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow shallowly into a light seed mix and keep warm and evenly moist. Plant hardened seedlings into a sunny, sharply drained spot and pinch tips to build a bushy shape. Rich purple daisies open across the plant through the summer season.

planting.note: A blend of blue, white and purple; sow indoors in spring.

directions: Sprinkle the mixed seed thinly and press in without deep burial. Grow the seedlings on in good light and transplant to sunny beds, edging, or containers. Enjoy a mingled show of blue, purple, and white daisies over the warm months.

planting.note: Compact mix for pots and edging; start indoors before last frost.

directions: Sow the small seed just below the surface and keep gently moist until it sprouts. Set compact plants close together for a solid carpet of bloom in containers or along path edges. A range of blue, mauve, pink, and white daisies fills in quickly during summer.

planting.note: Pastel mix; raise under cover and plant out after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed on a fine mix and hold at warm temperatures until germination. Harden off and plant in full sun with excellent drainage. Soft shades of blue, lilac, pink, and white daisies bloom generously all summer with occasional deadheading.

planting.note: Grown from cuttings and sold as plants; set out after frost.

directions: Plant nursery-grown plugs into free-draining soil or containers once frost has passed. Trim lightly through the season to keep the mound tidy and full. Masses of violet-blue daisies cover the fine foliage from spring until frost.

planting.note: Propagated from cuttings; plant out after the last frost.

directions: Set plants in gritty, well-drained soil in a warm, sunny position. Shear back after heavy flushes to renew the ferny foliage and prompt fresh buds. Soft mauve daisies smother the plant for months, peaking in spring and again in autumn.

planting.note: Sold as cutting-grown plants; plant after frost has passed.

directions: Plant into a sharply drained bed or a container of open, gritty mix. Give it full sun and trim spent flowers to keep the display tidy. Pink daisies that soften toward pale strawberry cover the low mound through the warm season.

planting.note: Australian hybrid grown from cuttings; plant out after frost.

directions: Establish plants in a warm, sunny spot with light, quick-draining soil. Cut back lightly after the main flush to maintain a compact, leafy cushion. Lavender-blue daisies with golden centers appear over a long spring-to-autumn season.

planting.note: Usually grown from cuttings or division; plant after frost.

directions: Plant in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun as an evergreen groundcover in mild areas. Divide clumps or take soft-tip cuttings in spring to make more plants. Mauve-blue daisies spangle the finely divided foliage over a very long season.

planting.note: Cutting-grown selection; plant out once frost danger ends.

directions: Set plants into open, well-drained soil in a sunny bed, rockery, or trough. Trim after flushes to keep the ferny cushion dense and encourage rebloom. Deep amethyst-purple daisies carpet the foliage for months in mild climates.

planting.note: Spreads by rhizomes; plant or divide after frost.

directions: Plant in a sunny, free-draining position where its spreading roots can knit into a low mat. Lift and divide congested clumps in spring to refresh and multiply plantings. Pale mauve to blue daisies rise above the narrow leaves through much of the warm season.

planting.note: Trailing species grown from cuttings; plant after frost.

directions: Plant along the front of a bed, a bank, or the edge of a container where the stems can trail and spread. Keep the soil gritty and never waterlogged, and shear lightly to renew growth. Bright pink daisies with yellow centers appear over a long spring-to-summer display.

planting.note: Shrubby island species from cuttings; plant after frost.

directions: Plant this bushier daisy in free-draining soil in full sun and shelter it from hard frost. Tip-prune young plants to build a rounded, leafy shrublet. Pale mauve to white daisies open over the fleshy foliage across the warm months.

planting.note: Cutting-grown hybrid; plant out after the last frost.

directions: Plant into light, quick-draining soil in a sunny bed or a hanging container. Shear back spent flowers to keep the ferny mound compact and blooming. A soft haze of pale pink daisies covers the plant through the warm season.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant to the garden after the last frost has passed.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface of a warm seed mix and keep it evenly damp until the seedlings appear. Give plants dappled light and shelter from harsh afternoon sun for the best flower color. Small blue-violet flowers with pale throats open along the branching stems through the warm months.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors about eight weeks early and set out compact plants after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on warm, moist soil and leave it uncovered so light can reach it. This tidy, slightly sticky-stemmed species suits pots and edging and needs little pinching. Bright blue flowers with white eyes cover the low mounds from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors in late winter and plant out when frost danger has passed.

directions: Sprinkle the seed on the surface of a warm mix and mist gently, keeping it uncovered for good germination. This free-branching selection needs only a single early pinch to fill out containers and beds. Lavender-blue flowers with white centers arrive early and keep coming until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Start indoors roughly ten weeks before the last frost for early bloom.

directions: Leave the fine seed uncovered on warm, damp soil and hold steady moisture until sprouts emerge. Grow in bright, partly shaded spots and water evenly to prevent wilting in heat. Deep marine-blue blooms cover the compact plants from summer through fall.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant after the danger of frost.

directions: Press seed lightly onto the surface of a warm seed tray and keep it moist and uncovered. This white-flowered form brightens shady containers and pairs well with the blue selections. Crisp white blooms open steadily from early summer until frost cuts the plants back.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and set young plants out once nights turn mild.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on a warm, evenly moist mix and do not cover, as light speeds sprouting. This blend gives a mix of blue, lavender, and white flowers on tidy, well-branched plants. Enjoy the varied colors together in beds and pots from summer through the first frost.

planting.note: Raise dwarf seedlings indoors and plant out after frost for edging and pots.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of warm soil and keep it damp and uncovered until it sprouts. This naturally compact dwarf needs no pinching and stays neat in small containers. Bright blue flowers smother the low mounds all season until frost.

planting.note: Start indoors about nine weeks early and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Leave the seed uncovered on a warm, moist mix and keep humidity steady during germination. This dwarf white form makes a clean edging and needs little grooming. Pure white blooms cover the tidy plants from summer into fall.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants from cuttings after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant nursery-grown starts at the same depth they sat in their pots and water them in well. This vegetative selection is grown from cuttings rather than seed and thrives in shade to part sun. Purple-blue flowers pour over the mounding plants nonstop from spring until frost with no deadheading needed.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings outdoors once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Set container-grown plants into rich, well-drained soil and keep them evenly moist as they establish. Grown from cuttings, this white-flowered form shines in shaded beds and hanging baskets. Clean white blooms cover the trailing mounds all season without any deadheading.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and plant out compact seedlings after frost.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface of a warm mix and press gently without covering it. This dwarf, bushy selection is well suited to pots, baskets, and front-of-border planting. Vivid sapphire-blue flowers with white eyes blanket the plants from summer until the first frost.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost, or start indoors a few weeks earlier for earlier bloom.

directions: Sow seeds about a quarter to half inch deep and keep the soil evenly moist until sprouts appear. Pinch young plants once to encourage bushy growth and deadhead spent blooms to keep flowers coming through the season. Daisy-like orange and yellow flowers open roughly six to eight weeks from sowing and continue until hard frost.

planting.note: A tough wild species best direct sown in cool weather in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Scatter seed shallowly on open, well-drained ground and press it lightly into the soil. This smaller wild relative tolerates poor soil and drought once established and often self-sows freely. Small pale yellow to orange flowers appear in spring and again in cool autumn weather.

planting.note: Sow in cool spring soil or start indoors for a taller cutting-garden mix.

directions: Plant seeds a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to give each plant room to branch. This taller strain produces long, sturdy stems in shades of orange, gold, apricot, and cream that are ideal for bouquets. Cut blooms in the morning when petals have just opened to encourage more flowers.

planting.note: A compact, dwarf mix good for edging, containers, and small beds.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep moist; seedlings emerge quickly in cool soil. This dwarf strain forms tidy mounds of fully double flowers in mixed gold, orange, and apricot tones. Deadhead regularly and it will bloom steadily from early summer, thriving in pots and border edges.

planting.note: An early, uniform dwarf series bred for pots and mass bedding.

directions: Start seeds indoors or sow directly a quarter inch deep in cool weather. This compact series flowers early and uniformly in bright orange and yellow, making it a reliable choice for containers. Remove faded heads to extend the long bloom period.

planting.note: A tall heirloom prized for cutting and its dark-backed petals.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in cool soil and thin to give the tall stems support. Rich orange petals are washed with a bronze-red reverse, giving cut arrangements a warm, antique glow. Harvest stems as buds open fully and keep cutting to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Grown for its vivid, quilled petals with contrasting dark centers.

directions: Plant seeds shallowly and keep the bed evenly moist during germination. Flowers show glowing orange, rolled petals set against deep maroon-brown centers for strong color contrast. Deadhead often and cut freely for vases to keep the display going.

planting.note: An heirloom with distinctive spiky, cactus-like quilled petals.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings once they have a few true leaves. The double flowers have narrow, rolled orange petals that give a starburst, cactus-flowered look. Pinch early for bushier plants and deadhead to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: A classic deep-orange double, popular for beds and herb gardens.

directions: Direct sow in cool spring soil about a quarter inch deep and keep moist. Large fully double blooms open in a rich, saturated orange that reads well from a distance. The edible petals are often dried, and steady deadheading extends the bloom.

planting.note: A soft-yellow selection that pairs well with orange forms.

directions: Sow seeds a quarter inch deep in cool weather and thin the seedlings. Clear lemon-yellow double flowers bring a lighter tone to the border and cutting garden. Deadhead spent blooms and the plants will flower from early summer into fall.

planting.note: An unusual pale, creamy calendula with a dark eye.

directions: Plant shallowly and keep evenly moist until germination. The creamy ivory petals are backed with a soft bronze reverse and centered by a dark disk, an uncommon look for calendula. Deadhead to encourage a long run of these subtle blooms.

planting.note: A crested, anemone-flowered mix with a raised central cushion.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in cool soil and thin to allow branching. Each flower has a ring of flat outer petals surrounding a raised, quilled center cushion in contrasting shades. This crested form makes a striking cut flower; keep cutting to prolong bloom.

planting.note: A warm mix with red-tipped and red-backed petals.

directions: Direct sow shallowly and keep the soil moist through germination. Double blooms in orange and gold are edged and backed with mahogany-red for a rich, two-toned effect. Deadhead frequently and the plants will bloom heavily through the cool season.

planting.note: Muted buff and cream tones with dusky red reverses, a favorite for florists.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in cool weather and thin seedlings for airflow. Soft petals in buff, cream, and pale apricot are backed with dusky maroon, giving a vintage color palette. Cut stems as flowers open and remove old heads to keep new buds coming.

planting.note: Warm salmon-apricot petals with bronze undersides.

directions: Plant seeds shallowly and keep evenly moist until they sprout. Blooms open in glowing apricot and salmon tones with a coppery-bronze reverse, a warm alternative to standard orange. Deadhead regularly and cut for arrangements to extend flowering.

planting.note: A frilly mix with pink-tinged, crested flowers.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings once established. Ruffled gold and apricot flowers carry pink-flushed petal edges and a crested center for extra texture. Keep the plants deadheaded and they will bloom over a long season.

planting.note: An artist's palette of soft apricot, buff, and orange shades.

directions: Direct sow in cool soil about a quarter inch deep and thin as needed. This tall mix blends muted apricot, cream, buff, and orange tones prized by flower arrangers. Harvest stems as blooms open and keep cutting to encourage a steady supply.

planting.note: Soft peach-buff blooms popular in the cutting garden.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool spring soil and keep moist until germination. Gentle peach, buff, and cream tones make this an elegant filler for bouquets. Deadhead spent flowers and cut often to keep the plants producing.

planting.note: A high-resin strain selected for herbal and skincare use.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin to give plants room. Bred for high resin content, its pale yellow to orange single and semi-double flowers are favored for salves and teas. Harvest whole flower heads on a dry morning when fully open and dry them promptly.

planting.note: A very compact dwarf series with dark-centered double flowers.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep moist; this dwarf series is well suited to pots and front-of-border planting. Double orange and yellow flowers each carry a dark central eye and sit neatly above compact foliage. Deadhead to keep the tidy mounds blooming.

planting.note: A shorter, single-flowered form with a relaxed cottage look.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep in cool weather and thin seedlings. The simple single flowers in yellow, orange, and cream have an easy, informal charm and draw pollinators. Deadhead often and the plants will bloom freely all season.

planting.note: A wide-ranging mix with strongly contrasting petal reverses.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool soil and keep the bed evenly moist until germination. This variable mix produces blooms in pink, apricot, buff, and orange, each with a darker reverse that flashes as the petals move. Cut and deadhead regularly to keep the range of colors coming.

planting.note: A novelty form with a green center surrounded by orange petals.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings once they establish. Bright orange petals surround an unusual green central tuft, giving an eye-catching, offbeat bloom. Deadhead to prolong flowering and cut stems for novelty in arrangements.

planting.note: A uniform bedding series with dark-centered double flowers.

directions: Sow shallowly or start indoors for early, even bloom. The double flowers come in orange and yellow with contrasting dark centers on well-branched, uniform plants. Deadhead spent heads to keep the bedding display fresh through the cool season.

planting.note: A traditional German medicinal strain with deep orange blooms.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep in cool soil and keep moist. This old herbal selection produces abundant deep-orange semi-double to double flowers used in traditional skin preparations. Pick open heads on a dry morning for drying, and deadhead to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: An improved deep-orange strain bred for uniform double blooms.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to allow branching. This selection yields large, fully double flowers in a consistent deep orange on strong stems. Cut for bouquets and deadhead often to keep the plants flowering.

planting.note: A fully double, incurved form resembling a small chrysanthemum.

directions: Direct sow a quarter inch deep in cool weather and keep evenly moist. The densely petaled, incurved orange flowers have a rounded, chrysanthemum-like form. Deadhead spent blooms and cut for vases to extend the flowering period.

planting.note: A muted cream-and-coffee toned selection favored by florists.

directions: Sow shallowly in cool soil and thin seedlings for good airflow. Soft cream petals are brushed with coffee-brown reverses and a dark center for a subtle, moody palette. Cut as flowers open and deadhead to keep new buds coming.

planting.note: A cold-tolerant series bred for autumn sowing and winter bloom in mild areas.

directions: Sow in late summer or early autumn so plants establish before cold sets in. This hardy series flowers through mild winters in shades of cream, apricot, and orange where frosts are light. Deadhead through the season and it will bloom on into spring in temperate climates.

planting.note: A well-branched, uniform pot and bedding series.

directions: Start seeds indoors a few weeks before the last frost and transplant into pots or beds after hardening off. This series is bred for compact, uniform, well-branched plants with double flowers in solid orange and yellow. Deadhead to keep the container display neat and long-blooming.

planting.note: A vigorous cut-flower strain; start early indoors for the longest picking season.

directions: Sow shallowly in cell trays and transplant after frost into rich, moisture-retentive ground. This wilt-tolerant series carries long, sturdy stems topped with double semi-globular flowers prized by florists. Cut stems in the cool of the morning when a few outer petals have opened for the best vase life.

planting.note: The violet-blue selection of the popular Matsumoto florist strain.

directions: Start seed indoors and press it lightly onto the surface without deep burial. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and stake taller plants in exposed sites. The deep violet-blue double blooms appear in late summer and hold their color well when dried or used fresh.

planting.note: A compact, weather-tolerant bedding series good for borders and pots.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and moist until the seedlings emerge in a few weeks. This short, self-supporting strain resists wind and rain damage, so it needs no staking in beds or containers. Full double flowers cover the mounded plants through summer with little care beyond regular watering.

planting.note: An incurved, chrysanthemum-like cut-flower type with strong stems.

directions: Raise transplants indoors and set them out after the last frost into well-worked, fertile soil. The tightly incurved petals resemble a small chrysanthemum and sit on long, upright stems built for cutting. Harvest as the blooms reach two-thirds open for a long vase life in late summer.

planting.note: An heirloom giant-flowered type with shaggy, curled petals.

directions: Start seed early indoors to give this late, tall strain time to finish blooming. Plant into deep, rich soil and stake the stems, which can top three feet and carry large loosely curled flowers. The full, feathery blooms open in late summer into fall and are outstanding for large arrangements.

planting.note: A vintage type with long, gracefully reflexed and curled petals.

directions: Sow indoors and transplant seedlings into fertile ground in full sun after frost. The long, curled ray petals give each flower a soft, feathery, plume-like look on branching stems. Blooms carry through late summer and are lovely both in the garden and cut for the vase.

planting.note: A bushy, free-branching bouquet type covered in rounded double blooms.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it evenly moist until sprouts appear. This many-branched strain produces a heavy crop of small, rounded pompon-style flowers ideal for cutting whole sprays. Pick generously through summer, as regular harvesting keeps new buds coming.

planting.note: Neat, ball-shaped double flowers on tidy upright plants.

directions: Start indoors in trays and move outdoors after danger of frost has passed. The compact, rounded pompon flowers hold their form well and sit on straight, wiry stems. Cut them for small posies as they reach full color in mid to late summer.

planting.note: A tall florist strain with crested centers ringed by flat outer petals.

directions: Sow early under cover so the plants have a long season to mature. Give them rich soil, full sun, and support for the tall stems that carry large crested flowers. The raised central quills surrounded by a collar of broad petals make striking cut blooms in late summer.

planting.note: An early-flowering type with loosely curled petals on shorter plants.

directions: Press seed lightly into the surface and keep warm to speed germination. This early strain begins blooming sooner than the giants, making it useful in short-summer gardens. Its softly curled, semi-double flowers open from midsummer and respond well to deadheading.

planting.note: A quilled, spider-like type whose petals roll into fine tubes.

directions: Raise seedlings indoors and set them into fertile, well-drained soil after frost. The narrow, tightly rolled petals give each bloom a distinctive quilled, needle-like texture. Long stems make this a favorite for cutting when flowers open in late summer.

planting.note: A branching cut-flower strain with small, well-formed double flowers.

directions: Sow shallowly indoors and transplant after the weather settles into full sun. The freely branching plants yield many slender stems topped with neat double blooms. Cut sprays through summer for airy, long-lasting arrangements.

planting.note: A tall, fully double type with incurved petals like a small peony.

directions: Start indoors and grow on strong before planting out into rich soil in full sun. The dense, incurved petals form rounded double flowers that resemble compact peonies on sturdy stems. Blooms are excellent for cutting when picked nearly fully open in late summer.

planting.note: A tall, needle-petaled professional cut-flower series with large blooms.

directions: Sow indoors early and grow into strong transplants for the longest stems. Set plants in deep, fertile soil and provide netting or stakes to keep the tall stems upright. The large, quilled double flowers open in late summer and are bred for shipping and long vase life.

planting.note: An unusual lime-green pompon aster popular with florists.

directions: Start seed under cover and transplant after frost into full sun and rich soil. The tight, ball-shaped flowers open a fresh chartreuse-green that adds an unusual color to bouquets. Cut stems as the pompons firm up in late summer for a novel, long-lasting filler.

planting.note: A spidery, loosely quilled type with wavy, twisting petals.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep warm until seedlings appear, then grow on in bright light. Move plants outdoors after frost into fertile soil and full sun. The long, wavy, loosely rolled petals give the flowers a shaggy, informal look through late summer.

planting.note: An open, daisy-form aster with a yellow eye that draws pollinators.

directions: Sow indoors or direct in warm soil, covering the seed only lightly. The single, daisy-like flowers show an open golden center that attracts bees and butterflies. Blooms carry through late summer and reseed readily if a few heads are left to ripen.

planting.note: A dwarf, early bedding type with fully double mum-like flowers.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep evenly moist until germination. These short, weather-hardy plants need no staking and bloom early with dense, incurved double flowers. Grow them at the front of a bed or in pots for a long summer show.

planting.note: A very compact, early bedding strain for edging and containers.

directions: Sow shallowly and grow seedlings in bright light before setting out after frost. The low, uniform mounds carry full double flowers well above the foliage without support. It is one of the earliest asters to bloom, making it reliable in cooler regions.

planting.note: A very short, dense heirloom bedding aster ideal for edging.

directions: Press seed lightly onto the surface and keep warm and moist to germinate. This dwarf, cushion-like strain forms tidy mounds smothered in small double flowers. Use it as a low edging or in window boxes where it blooms through summer with little fuss.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well ahead of the last frost since vinca is slow, and set out only after soil has thoroughly warmed.

directions: Sow seed about an eighth inch deep and cover to exclude light, keeping the mix warm and barely moist until sprouts appear. Grow on the dry side and give full sun, as soggy soil quickly causes root and stem rot. Expect the pinwheel-shaped blooms from midsummer until frost once heat settles in.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant after nights stay warm; this series is bred for resistance to aerial phytophthora.

directions: Barely cover the seed and hold at warm temperatures in darkness until germination, then grow lean and bright. Space upright plants a foot apart for good airflow, which helps this disease-resistant line shrug off wet-summer diseases. Broad flat flowers open across the mounded canopy from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Set out warm-loving transplants once the soil is reliably above sixty degrees.

directions: Press seed onto the surface with a light cover and keep it warm and dark until it emerges. Give full sun and let the soil dry between waterings to keep the plants healthy. Soft apricot flowers with a contrasting eye cover the mound through the hottest months.

planting.note: Best transplanted into baskets or containers after frost danger passes and warmth arrives.

directions: Start seed warm in darkness and pot up into hanging baskets or wide containers. Trailing stems spill over the edge, so let containers dry slightly between drinks to avoid rot. Cascading flowers cover the stems from early summer until the first frost.

planting.note: Transplant into baskets after the weather turns dependably warm.

directions: Sow warm and covered from light, then grow bright and on the dry side. The trailing habit suits window boxes and baskets, where lean watering prevents disease. White petals marked with a bold rose eye trail over the rim all summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early and plant out into warm beds for the biggest flowers.

directions: Cover seed lightly to block light and keep the mix warm until it sprouts. This series is grown for oversized blooms on a well-branched upright plant, so give it full sun and steady heat. Extra-large flowers cover the mound from early summer through frost.

planting.note: Move transplants outdoors only after the soil is warm to the touch.

directions: Germinate seed warm and in darkness, then grow in bright light with restrained watering. The deep saturated red flowers are unusually large for vinca and hold up in summer heat. Bloom continues nonstop from summer until the first cold nights.

planting.note: An early, uniform series; start indoors and set out after frost danger ends.

directions: Sow seed just below the surface, cover from light, and keep warm until emergence. Grow bright and lean, spacing plants for airflow to head off disease. Large rounded flowers with overlapping petals bloom heavily from summer into fall.

planting.note: Transplant into warm beds once nights stay mild.

directions: Keep seed warm and dark until it germinates, then grow on in strong light. Water only when the soil dries to protect the roots in humid weather. True red flowers cover the compact plant nonstop through the heat of summer.

planting.note: A compact bedding series; start indoors and plant out after the soil warms.

directions: Barely cover the seed to shut out light and keep it warm during germination. Grow bright and avoid overwatering, which is the main cause of failure in vinca. Large flowers, many with a contrasting eye, cover the tidy mound all season.

planting.note: Set transplants out once frost is past and the ground is warm.

directions: Sow warm and cover from light until the seedlings appear, then grow lean and sunny. Give the plants room so leaves dry quickly after rain. White flowers centered with a bright rose-red eye bloom steadily into autumn.

planting.note: A spreading series for baskets and ground cover; transplant after warm weather settles.

directions: Germinate seed warm and in darkness, then move seedlings to baskets or open beds. The low, spreading stems fill wide spaces and shed water fast in the sun. Large flowers blanket the trailing growth from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Grow warm indoors and plant into containers once nights stay mild.

directions: Keep seed warm and dark until it sprouts, then transplant into baskets or window boxes. The semi-trailing habit mounds and then spills over the sides. Rounded overlapping flowers cover the plant through the whole warm season.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after frost for the series' unusual smoky colors.

directions: Cover seed to exclude light and keep it warm until germination, then grow bright and lean. This series is prized for its inky, airbrushed color patterns held on sturdy mounds. Deep cherry-red flowers with a dark inner smudge bloom from summer to frost.

planting.note: Transplant into warm beds once the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed warm and in the dark, then grow on in full sun with careful watering. The unusual orange tones deepen in strong light and hot weather. Papaya-orange flowers with a rich red center appear all summer long.

planting.note: Set out well-hardened plants after the soil has warmed.

directions: Keep the seed warm and covered from light until it emerges, then grow bright. Hot sites bring out the deepest tangerine tones and keep the plants blooming. Orange flowers washed with a darker eye cover the mound into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant once nights are dependably warm.

directions: Germinate warm in darkness, then grow the seedlings hard in bright light. The smoky purple-blue color is unusual for vinca and reads best in full sun. Dusky violet flowers with a darker center bloom nonstop through the heat.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out into warm beds after frost.

directions: Cover seed to block light and hold it warm until it sprouts, then grow bright and lean. Prized for its saturated near-black petal color with a small white eye. The deep purple flowers glow in full sun from summer until frost.

planting.note: An early, large-flowered series; transplant after the soil warms.

directions: Sow seed warm and dark, then grow the seedlings on in strong light. Space plants for airflow and water sparingly to keep them disease-free. Large early flowers cover the compact plant from summer into fall.

planting.note: A vigorous spreading series for baskets; plant out after warm nights arrive.

directions: Keep seed warm and covered from light until germination, then pot into baskets. The spreading stems fill containers quickly and drape over the edge. Flowers cover the trailing growth from early summer until frost.

planting.note: A heat-loving upright series; set out only after the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Cover seed lightly to exclude light and keep it warm until it emerges. Bred to thrive in intense heat and humidity, it wants full sun and lean water. Rounded flowers cover the vigorous upright plant from summer to frost.

planting.note: Sold as vegetative transplants; plant into warm beds or baskets after frost.

directions: Set the vegetatively grown transplant into well-drained soil or a container after nights turn warm. Its small, densely packed flowers cover a mounding, spreading plant that thrives in heat and humidity. Give full sun and lean water, and it blooms without deadheading from spring through fall.

planting.note: A vegetative double-flowered form; transplant after the weather is dependably warm.

directions: Plant the rooted transplant into a container or warm bed once frost danger has passed. The fully double, rosette-like blooms are unusual for vinca and cover a heat-tolerant spreading plant. Grow in full sun with restrained watering for continuous bloom until frost.

planting.note: A compact heat-tolerant series; transplant into warm beds after frost.

directions: Sow seed warm and covered from light, then grow bright and on the dry side. The tidy plants take full sun and reflected heat in beds and containers alike. Bright flowers keep coming from early summer straight through to frost.

planting.note: Grown for very large flowers; start indoors and plant out after the soil warms.

directions: Cover seed to shut out light and keep it warm until germination, then grow in strong sun. The oversized flowers overlap to nearly hide the foliage on a vigorous mound. Give lean water for airflow and enjoy big blooms from summer to frost.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the soil has warmed and all frost danger has passed.

directions: Press seed barely below the surface, about an eighth of an inch deep, and keep the bed evenly warm and moist until sprouts appear. Give plants full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and avoid overwatering once established. Silvery-pink feathery spikes rise through summer; cut or leave them as the tips begin to open.

planting.note: Start indoors about a month before the last frost, then set out after nights stay warm.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm cells and transplant without disturbing the roots, as celosia resents checks to its growth. Plant into full sun and fertile, well-drained ground once weather is settled. The velvety brain-like combs color up by midsummer and hold for weeks; harvest for drying when the crest is fully formed but still firm.

planting.note: Begin indoors four to six weeks ahead of the last spring frost.

directions: Cover the tiny seed lightly and keep it warm; seedlings emerge quickly and dislike sitting cold and wet. Move to full sun and space to allow airflow around the fluffy plumes. Feathery flame-shaped heads glow from early summer to frost and make long-lasting cut or dried stems.

planting.note: Direct sow after the soil warms, or start early indoors for a head start.

directions: Scatter seed on warm, well-drained soil and barely cover it, thinning seedlings to give each room to branch. This is the most heat-tolerant celosia and needs little pampering once rooted. Slim wheat-like spikes in pink, rose, and silver bloom nonstop into fall and dry beautifully.

scientific_name: Celosia argentea var. plumosa 'Fresh Look Red'

planting.note: Start indoors about five weeks before last frost for early bedding color.

directions: Surface-sow into warm cells and provide bright light as seedlings develop. This award-winning plumosa is self-branching and sheds spent plumes cleanly, so no deadheading is needed. Deep-red feathery heads keep coming from summer through frost in beds or containers.

scientific_name: Celosia argentea var. plumosa 'Fresh Look Yellow'

planting.note: Sow indoors roughly a month before setting out after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep the tray warm and evenly moist until germination. Give full sun and light, free-draining soil for the sturdiest plants. Bright golden-yellow plumes self-clean and bloom continuously with no deadheading through the heat of summer.

scientific_name: Celosia argentea var. plumosa 'Fresh Look Gold'

planting.note: Begin indoors four to six weeks before the frost-free date.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a warm, sterile mix and firm the seed in lightly. Transplant carefully into full sun once the weather settles. Warm golden plumes rise above tidy mounds and need no trimming to keep blooming until frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors a month or so ahead of the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm, then grow on in strong light to hold the color of the foliage. This variety pairs bronze-purple leaves with scarlet plumes for two-tone contrast. Plant in full sun and enjoy dense flower heads from summer to first frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors early since this vigorous type fills large containers.

directions: Start seed warm and grow the seedlings hard in bright light for stocky plants. This large, heat-loving selection carries fiery-red plumes above burgundy-flushed foliage. Give it full sun and space, and it will branch into a bold mound that blooms until frost.

scientific_name: Celosia argentea var. plumosa 'First Flame Red'

planting.note: Start indoors about five weeks before your last frost date.

directions: Press seed onto warm mix and keep bright and moist during sprouting. This compact, well-branched plumosa flowers early and holds neatly in packs. Set into full sun for a steady show of red plumes from early summer onward.

planting.note: Sow indoors four to six weeks before the last frost.

directions: Barely cover seed and provide warmth for prompt, even germination. This mid-tall plumosa series carries large, dense plumes on upright stems that suit beds or cutting. Plant in full sun and pinch nothing; blooms build through summer to a strong display.

planting.note: Start indoors early for a very quick, dwarf bedding crop.

directions: Surface-sow into warm cells and keep bright to avoid stretched seedlings. This is one of the earliest and shortest plumosa series, ideal for edging and small pots. Space closely in full sun for a carpet of little plumes in mixed jewel tones.
